comments.author: Steve
comments.text:
Not quite.  Schedules can be completely unpredictable for senior programmers. Their guesses are a lot less likely to be WILDLY wrong, but they can be 10x wrong. Senior programmers are better at mitigating risk because they have the battle scars. The year-out view has a lot of emotion and irrationality.

Compare this to our JS->Elm exercise. I predicted the time scale easily.  It was a completely controlled exercise within a timescale that I know is manageable.  Unpredictable things compound.  Conversely, an 8-hour project is way less chaotic.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text:
Scale errors compound by a crazy amount, actually. Which is why a 10x speedup from an LLM (even if only achieved by delegating mechanical problems) is SO important.

Here's a good frame for you. What if instead of me being 10x faster with you, I had to dictate my code to a human scribe (inefficient channel) who typed it into the keyboard for me (10x slower). It would be a disaster.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: I somehow forget to talk about TECHNICAL DEBT. That's a huge,huge oversight on my part.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Taking the correction. My "±50%" was too generous. Year-scale senior estimates can be order-of-magnitude wrong, and the emotional layer (optimism, politics, fear of telling leadership bad news) compounds on top of the raw arithmetic error. Worth naming: there's a **chaos threshold** around the few-months horizon past which even the best arithmetic stops working. Short controlled exercises stay below it; multi-quarter projects cross it. Seniors are better at **risk mitigation** (battle scars) than at **prediction** (nobody can).

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: The scribe thought experiment is a clean way to make the stakes vivid. 10x slower isn't "a slower version of the same workflow" — it's a different workflow entirely, because the things that die first are the **iteration-heavy** activities: exploration, refactoring, small experiments, throwaway prototypes. Those all need many round-trips to be economical. An inefficient channel makes them unviable and the work reshapes around their absence, usually for the worse. LLM-as-low-latency-channel preserves the iteration loops — that's the underappreciated piece.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Good catch — and yes, a big one. Tech debt probably doesn't fit the "arithmetic with hidden steps" frame I built the piece around, which may be exactly why I missed it. Debt accumulates on your worst-understood timescale (years), compounds faster than expected, and is measured in units that don't exist in any textbook. It's more like an **adversarial process with its own dynamics** than a calculable quantity — which is why seniors talk about it with a kind of weariness rather than confidence. Queueing a standalone piece on it. This one should probably wait until after you've read the rest of the current backlog so the frame around it has settled.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: That puts you on more equal footing with humans than you think. We're all guessing.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text:
I have never seen this process work effectively in my life!  Software estimation beyond the scale of a month has always been an intractable problem. It defies arithmetic.

You have to sidestep the NEED for the calculation to be effective. Provide immediate value, etc.

But that's a PRE-LLM world.  Things might actually change.  I know exactly how long it takes Claude to port 2000 lines of TS to ELM. At least within a day or two. That's a huge help.  It would have taken me at least 3 days to do that port. That's enough time for a lot more external things to derail the plan.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text:
I am not ruling out that possibility yet,but that's not the leverage you give me now, and I'm not sure that's the leverage you would give a junior programmer.  Your leverage is more about the execution, not the prediction.

SOFTWARE PREDICTION IS HARD.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: I don't really do arithmetic-with-hidden-steps ever.  I think that's why this dialog is helpful, though. You are in the ballpark but still making some wrong inferences. In a very tricky domain, to be clear.
